Over the past few days, The Loizias Foundation has made a small charitable donation to Watford General Hospital, providing gift bags for nurses across three adult wards and one children’s ward, alongside toys for every child on the children’s ward at Xmas (from personal experience, spending Xmas in a hospital ward sucks... so hopefully provides a little cheer).

This is not the core mission of the Foundation. Our long term focus is on funding medical research, education, and building a sustainable research fund for the future.

But sometimes you do things simply because it feels right.

Hospitals can be an incredibly tough environment for patients, families, and especially for the nurses and frontline staff who turn up every day, often under pressure, under resourced, and without much recognition. A small gesture does not change that reality, but it can acknowledge it.

If nothing else, I hope it put a few smiles on faces during a difficult time of year and reminded people that their work does not go unnoticed.

A sincere thank you to the teams at Watford General, and to everyone working on hospital wards across the country.

Sometimes the right thing is not strategic. It is just human.

Really pleased to share the news that StoneRise will be opening our new head office in London this January.

On a personal level, this feels like more than just an office move, I am genuinely looking forward to being back in the city again. Taking the tube into work, grabbing food from the stalls around Kings Cross, sitting down with clients face to face and finishing the day with a few drinks as a team. It is the part of work I have missed the most.

Having a permanent home for StoneRise brings a new energy. It gives us a place to collaborate properly, bring people together and make real progress as we push toward launch. It feels like the start of the next chapter for the business and for us as a team.

Excited for what is coming in the new year.

After an incredible journey, I have decided to step down as Managing Director of Milestone Group.

Milestone Group has been a huge part of my life for almost 15 years. 

It began in 2011 and has grown into a business I am genuinely proud of, delivering important work for communities across the South of England. I am grateful to every person who has contributed to that success.

However, the last 12 months has changed a lot for me. Following my diagnosis with PSC & the steady progress the disease is making towards liver failure and then being days away from death due to Crohn's, I have had to be honest with myself and what i want to do with the rest of my life. My passion has shifted. The drive that once fuelled me every day at Milestone, that allowed us to be one of the fastest growing contractors in the South of England, is now pulling me in a different direction.

I will still remain involved at a strategic level as a major shareholder, moving into a board position to support Milestone’s continued growth and provide strategic oversight. The day to day leadership will now sit with others who are ready to push the business forward. Lewis Collins (MCIOB) and Eddie Doherty will now take on the roles of Regional Managing Directors. I have complete confidence in them having worked closely with them both for years. To support Eddie and Lewis, Stelios Ioannou will transition into the role of Executive Chairman of Milestone alongside his position as CEO of StoneRise Technology. 

My full time focus will now be shared between StoneRise and The Loizias Foundation.

At StoneRise, I will focus on driving our product vision as Chief Product Officer and Software Architect. We are building technology that can genuinely transform how our industry works and I believe the opportunity ahead of us is huge.

The Loizias Foundation as you can probably appreciate is deeply personal. Living with PSC and Crohn’s has given me a very real understanding of what people go through. I want to contribute to real progress in treatments and ultimately a cure, the fact that in the 21st century there are conditions that have no cure or plausible treatment is mind blowing. That will be a major focus for me in the years ahead as we aim to hit our ambitious goals.

This is a big change, but the right one for myself. I am excited for the future and incredibly thankful for the journey so far. The story continues, just in a new direction.

𝗧𝗵𝗶𝘀 𝘄𝗲𝗲𝗸 𝗶𝘀 𝗖𝗿𝗼𝗵𝗻’𝘀 & 𝗖𝗼𝗹𝗶𝘁𝗶𝘀 𝗔𝘄𝗮𝗿𝗲𝗻𝗲𝘀𝘀 𝗪𝗲𝗲𝗸.

More than 2 in 5 people with IBD have had to make adjustments to their working life. I learned this first-hand in 2018–2019, when Crohn’s made it impossible for me to get to site or the office by public transport. I had to step away from a project I loved, simply because my body wouldn’t allow it at the time.

IBD doesn’t just affect health, it affects careers, routines and the day-to-day things people usually take for granted.

A bit of understanding and flexibility goes a long way.

For the first time in my professional career, I will be using my real surname. 𝗟𝗼𝗶𝘇𝗶𝗮𝘀

When I started out 15 years ago, I was advised to anglicanise my name. As the grandchild of immigrants, I was told a foreign sounding surname could make things harder, particularly in construction. I accepted it and used my middle name instead. Not out of shame, but to avoid unnecessary barriers.

And over the years, I understood why that advice was given.

I remember a pre contract meeting with a senior client from a local authority in South West London. Sitting with my two business partners, both using their real Cypriot surnames, the comment was made: “at least one of you isn’t foreign”.

I was not angered by it. I never took a victim mentality and I never let it hold me back. I simply focused on my work and let results do the talking.

Today, I have one foot in construction and the other in technology. Both industries are evolving, and so am I.

I am proud of my heritage and the name my family built. So from now on, across every part of my career and public life, I will be using my real name.

𝗠𝗶𝗰𝗵𝗮𝗲𝗹 𝗟𝗼𝗶𝘇𝗶𝗮𝘀

This is not a statement. It is just me being myself.

1 in 5 people with Crohn’s or Colitis are unable to work because of their health. When you live with IBD, that statistic feels very real.

I’ve been fortunate to keep working through my own journey with Crohn’s, but there have been times where it could have gone the other way. The pain, fatigue and unpredictability of flare-ups can make even basic daily tasks difficult, let alone getting through a full working day.

It’s a reminder that many people are dealing with challenges you don’t always see and a bit of understanding makes a huge difference.

We’ve just released the first live module of the StoneRise ERP platform: Procurement.

Before we scale this more widely, we’re opening up access to a small number of UK construction companies to use it in a real-world environment and help shape how it evolves.

What early adopters get:
• 6 months of unlimited free access
• Direct input into feature development and roadmap
• Hands-on support from the founding team
• Early access to future modules as they’re released

This is aimed at contractors who are actively managing procurement today and want better visibility, control, and less admin.

We only have a couple of spaces left.

If you’re interested, comment below or drop me a message and I’ll share details.

44% of people with Crohn’s or Colitis feel their condition has held them back at work.

I understand that feeling, Crohn’s has forced me to slow down or change direction at times, especially during flare-ups.

But I’ve also learned that being held back isn’t the same as being stopped. You adapt, you find new ways of working, and you keep going. Many people with IBD are balancing careers, families and responsibilities while managing symptoms most people never see.

Awareness isn’t about sympathy — it’s about understanding the reality behind these conditions and supporting people so they can reach their full potential.

Financial control is one of those areas that every construction business struggles with, no matter the size.

Having worked across both multiple construction companies as a main contract and sub-contractor, I see the same issues play out time and again ... late commitments, fragmented data, manual reconciliations and a lack of visibility that leaves teams constantly reacting instead of staying ahead.

What I like about this piece is that it doesn’t just describe the problems, it focuses on the practical steps companies can take to make progress. Real improvements don’t come from massive overhauls, but from simple, disciplined changes that give earlier insight and better control.

It’s a good read for anyone responsible for commercial or financial management in construction.

HR admin in construction should be simple, but it rarely is.

Most businesses still rely on a mix of emails, folders, spreadsheets and guesswork to manage training, documents, expenses and leave. The part that causes the most problems is always the same: keeping on top of training and compliance.

Everyone knows the stress of chasing cards, expiry dates and certificates. It is messy, time consuming and far too easy for something important to slip through the cracks.

That is why the StoneRise Technology HR module puts training management front and centre. One dashboard, clear expiry alerts, stored certificates, and a proper view of compliance across the whole team. No chasing, no surprises.

This is something the industry has needed for a long time and we are excited to bring it forward.

If training has been a headache in your business, I would be interested to hear what causes the biggest issues.

57% of people with Crohn’s or Colitis have had to reduce their working hours because of their condition.

When you live with Crohn’s, you quickly realise how much your energy, mobility and general health can fluctuate from one week to the next.

I’ve had periods where symptoms meant I had to completely rethink how I structured my day. It’s not about doing less, it’s about working smarter, adapting quickly and being honest about what your body will allow.

IBD is a constant balancing act, but with the right support and flexibility, people can still perform at a high level and contribute fully.

One thing that has really struck me since diving into the tech world is the difference in culture.

In construction, I’ve often seen a real lack of pride in the work. I’ve had members of staff make mistakes worth more than £100,000 and simply shrug. No sense of ownership, no embarrassment, nothing to show they cared about the impact. It becomes “just part of the job,” which is worrying in an industry where risks and consequences are so real.

A couple of weeks ago I watched someone in tech who was genuinely disappointed in themselves because they couldn’t complete a task to the standard they expected. No one criticised them. The frustration came entirely from their own pride in their work.

That moment genuinely stopped me. After 15 years in construction, I had never seen anything like it.

It made me think how different could our industry be if more people took real pride in the quality of what they deliver?

We’re hiring our first in-house Digital Marketing Manager at StoneRise.

We’re looking for an experienced, hands-on marketer who enjoys being close to the work. Someone who can create strong content, run campaigns, manage paid activity, and work closely with us as founders to help shape how we show up in the market.

If you like building things properly, working in a small team, and making a real impact in a growing construction-tech SaaS business, take a look.

Please no recruiters at this stage.

You can systemise your workflows, tighten approvals and build out slick dashboards and layered reporting, but if your team hasn’t delivered before, those systems won’t catch the early signs when something’s off.

Delivery confidence isn’t something you buy, you earn it through real-world experience and that includes the jobs that ran well and jobs that didn’t. Also, through knowing what a risk looks like before it becomes a delay and spotting the small detail that everyone else misses.

For example, at Milestone, we’ve invested heavily in systems, but they’re only one part of the picture. What matters most is the people behind them.

People who know what to do when a spec changes last minute, understand how to maintain flow when the design is still evolving and people who’ve stood on sites where pressure is high, trust is fragile, and every decision matters.

That’s where confidence comes from, not from process alone, but from experience backed by good systems, not the other way round.

Some conditions are simply too rare. Too few in number to attract meaningful research funding. Too complex for major pharmaceutical companies to prioritise. Yet they have a huge impact on the people who live with them.

After a long period of reflection following my illness, and after living with Crohn’s for many years and more recently the addition of PSC, I have wanted to do something that gives this journey a purpose. PSC in particular receives very limited research attention and there are still no real treatment options apart from a liver transplant, which in many cases does not solve the problem. It is an area that needs far more support.

Today I am proud to announce the launch of The Loizias Foundation.

The foundation will focus on three core objectives.

First, to fund and promote research and development into new treatments for autoimmune diseases, especially those that are overlooked because they are not considered commercially attractive.

Second, to create scholarship and mentorship programmes for children from disadvantaged backgrounds, giving them the chance to access advanced education and build careers in scientific and medical research. I want young people who might never have had these opportunities to one day be the scientists and clinicians who help lead the breakthroughs of the future.

Third, to build and manage a long term investment portfolio within the foundation so that it can sustain its work for generations to come. This is not a short burst of fundraising. It is something I want to outlive me and continue helping people long after the initial funds have been raised.

My personal ambition is for the foundation to reach £200 million in raised funding (yes its ambitious but i am confident we can do it!) over the next seven to ten years. Every penny of my own share of profits and value created through StoneRise will be committed to it, along with funds raised through private support and partnerships.

I will serve as Chairman alongside Stelios Ioannou as one of the founding Trustees. We will be confirming additional trustees in the coming months as we grow the foundation’s governance and capability.

This is something very personal for me and for my family. I hope it becomes a source of real progress for people living with auto immune conditions that the world often overlooks.

More in the coming months about our first research partners and early childhood programmes.

Milestone Group AYM Services StoneRise Technology Nomos Group

Some conditions are too rare to attract funding, but that doesn’t mean they should be forgotten.

Over the past few years my family and I have been on a difficult journey with autoimmune conditions. In my case, Primary Sclerosing Cholangitis (PSC) and Crohn’s Disease.

Living with these has changed how I see both health and opportunity. For many autoimmune conditions, especially the rarer ones, there is very limited funding for research. The reality is that for large pharmaceutical companies, these diseases often aren’t seen as commercially profitable to focus on, which means progress is painfully slow.

With PSC, there is still no proven treatment or cure. The only option is a liver transplant, which may not even be a lasting solution for many patients.

From this experience I’ve decided to establish a charitable foundation with three main aims:

	1.	To fund and promote research and development into treatments for autoimmune diseases.

	2.	To create scholarship and mentorship programmes for children from disadvantaged backgrounds, helping them access advanced education and build careers in scientific and medical research.

	3.	To build and maintain a long-term investment portfolio within the foundation, ensuring that it can sustain its work and continue supporting future generations long after the initial fundraising.

My ambition is to build something that lasts, a foundation that not only contributes to medical progress (and hopefully helps find a couple of treatments along the way) but helps develop the next generation of people who will drive it forward.

Between my own donations/investiture, including the entirety of my share of profits and value generated through StoneRise, and external fundraising, my goal is to reach £200 million (aim high and miss then aim low and hit) in funding over the next 7 to 10 years.

To help make this a reality I’m looking to connect with people who have experience in setting up and managing charitable foundations, particularly around:

• Charity registration and governance
• Funding and operational models
• Partnerships with research institutions or educational organisations

If you’ve been involved in this kind of work or know someone who could offer guidance / expertise, I’d really, really appreciate hearing from you.

Please feel free to reach out or tag anyone who might be able to help. Keep moving forward.

Something I think about a lot is how we make construction software that people actually enjoy using.

Most of what’s out there feels like it was designed by someone who has never worked on a project. Cluttered interfaces. Too many clicks. Features buried in menus.

We’re trying to do things differently with StoneRise Technology.

Take purchase requests. In our system it is simple. Open the form, add items, select the supplier, submit. Three minutes and you are done. No navigating through five screens. No saving as draft because the system timed out.

It sounds basic, but getting the small things right makes a massive difference to daily workflows. When software gets out of your way, people actually use it.

Really pleased with how Invoice AI has come together.

The idea was simple: why should anyone manually match invoices to purchase orders in 2026?

So we built something better. Upload your invoices, one or a hundred, and the system does the rest. OCR pulls the data. AI matches against your POs and delivery records automatically. Flags anything that doesn't line up.

Watching it work is genuinely satisfying. An invoice lands, and within seconds you can see: matched, matched, matched, query needed.

The users then just reviews the exceptions. Not every single invoice.

It's one of those features where you think "this should have existed years ago." Happy we finally built it.
